paper_title: Peripheral Macular Endothelial Dystrophy: Clinical, Histopathologic, Genetic and Functional Characterization.
raw_limitation_text: To report a CHST6-associated corneal endothelial dystrophy. Prospective observational case series. Thirty-five individuals from seven families, including 13 affected individuals exhibiting corneal epithelial and stromal edema, peripheral posterior corneal macular opacities, and endothelial guttae, as well as 22 unaffected family members. Whole-exome sequencing was performed in 3 families and Sanger sequencing of CHST6 was performed in all individuals. Histological examination of Descemet membrane (DM) excised at the time of endothelial keratoplasty was performed for three probands. Serum keratan sulfate (KS) levels were measured in members of six families. Functional analysis of identified mutations was performed using CHST6 promoter containing CHST6 expression vector in human keratocytes (HK) and corneal endothelial cells (HCEnC). Clinical phenotype; genetic analysis; functional analysis of identified CHST6 mutations; serum KS levels; histologic examinations of DM. All affected individuals demonstrated peripheral macular opacities at the level of DM. Visually significant corneal edema in affected individuals was successfully managed by endothelial keratoplasty. Genetic analysis demonstrated a rare CHST6 promoter mutation (c.-690G>C) in the homozygous state in affected individuals from three families and in the compound heterozygous state with a CHST6 coding mutation (p.R211Q, p.Y268C or p.P280L) in affected individuals from the other four families. In silico analysis predicted c.-690G>C to be a regulatory variant, located at the RNA polymerase II binding site. Functional analysis in vitro demonstrated that c.-690G>C leads to increased KS sulfation in the corneal endothelium and DM, with no change of KS sulfation in keratocytes. Histologic examination of DM from affected individuals revealed elevated levels of sulfated and non-sulfated KS in DM and endothelium, consistent with the functional analysis. Minimum changes in serum sulfated KS levels were observed in affected individuals. We suggest the name Peripheral macular endothelial dystrophy (PMED) to describe this dystrophy that is characterized by peripheral posterior corneal macular opacities and endothelial dysfunction without stromal haze or opacities. Given that both PMED and macular corneal dystrophy are associated with promoter and coding region mutations in CHST6, we propose that they be categorized as CHST6-associated corneal dystrophies.
limitations: The study is limited by its small sample size of 35 individuals from seven families.
research_gaps: There is a lack of understanding of the long-term outcomes of patients with PMED after endothelial keratoplasty.
future_work: Future research should focus on larger cohort studies to validate the findings and explore the full spectrum of CHST6-associated corneal dystrophies.
methodological_weaknesses: The reliance on whole-exome sequencing in only three families may not capture all genetic variations associated with PMED.
